In video-related tasks, multiple frames need to be sampled to represent the video effectively.

Standard protocol involves training the same network with different frames separately to obtain multiple models with different performances and computations.
Challenges arise when applying these networks on edge devices due to the multiplication of parameters if all models are stored, and the time cost of downloading and offloading models to switch them.

Employing a single network that is trained at a certain frame number for inference cannot resist the variance of frame numbers in real scenarios, showing the importance of considering frame numbers in model design.

Training the model with a high frame number (high temporal frequency) and directly evaluating it at fewer frames (low temporal frequency) is mentioned as a naive and straightforward solution to adjust the cost.

A comparison is made between training the model with a high frame number and evaluating it at fewer frames versus using Separated Training (ST) which trains the model at different temporal frequencies individually to address the cost adjustment.
Separated Training (ST) is compared to Temporal Frequency Deviation, showing performance gaps between the two methods which are affected by the frame number used in training.
The existing phenomenon of Temporal Frequency Deviation leads to the proposal of a general framework called Frame Flexible Network (FFN) to address the issue with flexibility in evaluation.
Frame Flexible Network (FFN) utilizes Multi-Frequency Alignment (MFAL) during training to learn temporal frequency invariant representations for robustness towards frame change.
Multi-Frequency Adaptation (MFAD) is presented to strengthen the representation abilities of the sub-networks in Frame Flexible Network (FFN), contributing to the overall performance improvement.

Batch Normalization calculates statistics such as means and variances of the aggregated features, which can vary based on the number of channels and frame numbers.
The adjustment in model structure is not considered in the context, but the focus is on the impact of different frame numbers on normalization shifting.
The question arises whether differing frame numbers will cause normalization shifting in the model, especially when trained with high temporal frequency and evaluated with low temporal frequency.
The input of Batch Normalization (BN) can be the intermediate feature xL when training the model with high temporal frequency vH and evaluating it with low temporal frequency vL.
There is a discrepancy in Batch Normalization (BN) statistics observed at different frame numbers, indicating a potential impact on feature aggregation.
Statistics such as means and variances (µH, σH 2) are calculated from the data distribution of a particular temporal frequency vH.
Statistics are calculated separately for models trained with different temporal frequencies (vL and vH), showcasing the impact of data-dependent factors on the model.
The discrepancy in BN statistics at different frame numbers is attributed to data inherent divergence, leading to Temporal Frequency Deviation.
Layer Normalization (LN) has been commonly used in Transformer-based models, highlighting its significance in model optimization.
The statistics of Layer Normalization (LN) are calculated based on the data distribution, contributing to model optimization in Transformer-based architectures.
LN has been widely used in Transformer-based models and its statistics are calculated in a similar way with BN which is related to the data distribution.
The discrepancy of LN statistics is considered one of the reasons for Temporal Frequency Deviation on Transformer-based models.

According to our analysis in Sec. 3, normalization shifting is one of the reasons which leads to Temporal Frequency Deviation.
Formally, we denote the intermediate features for vL, vM, and vH as xL, xM, and xH, respectively, and provide specialized normalization for different input sequences.
Consequently, we utilize KL divergence loss to involve pL and pM in the computational graph and update the parameters of FL (·) and FM (·).
Optimizing Eq. 4 will enforce the predictions of student (pL and pM ) and teacher (pH) networks to be as similar as possible and transfer the good knowledge from FH (·) to FL (·) and FM (·).
Considering the two losses in a uniform manner, we update the parameters of FFN by L = LCE + λ · LKL, where λ is an introduced hyperparameter to balance the two.
FFN parameters are updated by balancing the cross-entropy loss with the Kullback-Leibler divergence. LCE provides inter-class supervisory information.

Given a frame number n at inference phase, we will calculate the frame difference with L, M and H, and activate the sub-network with the minimal difference for validation. If the frame difference is the same for two sub-networks, we will choose the one which corresponds higher frame number by default.
